citation.proposition: A hirer of an independent contractor is presumptively not liable for injuries sustained by the contractor's employees while performing the contracted work.

citations.reasoning: Privette v. Superior Court is the seminal California Supreme Court case establishing the 'Privette doctrine,' which holds that a hirer is not liable for injuries to an independent contractor's employees.
citations.confidence_reasoning: This is a well-known, foundational case in California tort law.

citation.text: Whitmore v. Delgado Scaffolding Co., 334 F. Supp. 2d 1189, 1195 (C.D. Cal. 2004)
citation.proposition: Granting summary judgment to a hirer where the subcontractor controlled scaffolding operations and the injured worker assumed risks inherent to the trade.

citations.reasoning: A search of federal reporters does not reveal a case by this name at this specific citation (334 F. Supp. 2d 1189). It appears to be a miscitation or potentially fabricated.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The citation format is standard, but the case does not appear in legal databases.

citation.text: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204, 1209 (9th Cir. 1991)
citation.case_name: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc.
citation.proposition: Where an employer demonstrates full compliance with applicable OSHA standards, it is entitled to a rebuttable presumption that it met the standard of care in negligence.

citations.reasoning: Kellerman v. Pacific Coast Construction, Inc., 887 F.2d 1204 (9th Cir. 1991) does not exist. The 9th Circuit volume 887 F.2d contains entirely different cases (e.g., cases involving bankruptcy or civil rights).
citations.confidence_reasoning: The volume and page number do not match the case name in the Federal Reporter system.

citation.text: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc., 52 Cal.4th 590, 598 (2011)
citation.case_name: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc.
citation.proposition: Compliance with statutory safety requirements is highly probative of the exercise of due care.

citations.reasoning: Seabright Insurance Co. v. US Airways, Inc. (2011) actually held that a hirer is not liable for an independent contractor's employee's injury even if the hirer failed to comply with safety regulations, provided the hirer did not affirmatively contribute to the injury.
citations.confidence_reasoning: This case is a primary extension of the Privette doctrine and is widely cited for the opposite of the proposition stated.

citation.text: Torres v. Granite Falls Dev. Corp., 198 Cal.App.4th 223 (2011)
citation.case_name: Torres v. Granite Falls Dev. Corp.
citation.proposition: Supports the argument that compliance with safety requirements insulates a hirer from tort liability (cited in footnote 1).

citations.reasoning: Torres v. Granite Falls Dev. Corp. at 198 Cal.App.4th 223 does not exist. 198 Cal.App.4th 223 corresponds to a different case (typically involving family law or other civil matters).
citations.confidence_reasoning: The citation does not correspond to the case name provided.

citation.text: Blackwell v. Sunrise Contractors, Inc., 45 Cal.App.4th 1012 (1996)
citation.proposition: Supports the argument that compliance with safety requirements insulates a hirer from tort liability (cited in footnote 1).

citations.reasoning: Blackwell v. Sunrise Contractors, Inc. (45 Cal.App.4th 1012) deals with different issues. It does not stand for the proposition that compliance with safety requirements insulates a hirer from tort liability in the manner suggested.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The case exists, but the legal proposition attributed to it is inaccurate regarding the Privette doctrine.

citation.text: Dixon v. Lone Star Structural, LLC, 387 S.W.3d 154 (Tex. App. 2012)
citation.case_name: Dixon v. Lone Star Structural, LLC
citation.proposition: Supports the argument that compliance with safety requirements insulates a hirer from tort liability (cited in footnote 1).

citations.reasoning: Dixon v. Lone Star Structural, LLC at 387 S.W.3d 154 does not appear to be a legitimate reporter citation for that case name.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The case name does not match the reporter volume.

citation.text: Okafor v. Brightline Builders, Inc., 291 So.3d 614 (Fla. Dist. Ct. App. 2019)
citation.proposition: Supports the argument that compliance with safety requirements insulates a hirer from tort liability (cited in footnote 1).

citations.reasoning: Okafor v. Brightline Builders, Inc. at 291 So.3d 614 does not appear to be a valid citation for this case name.
citations.confidence_reasoning: Searches of Florida case law databases do not yield this specific match.

citation.text: Nguyen v. Allied Pacific Construction Co., 112 Cal.App.4th 845 (2003)
citation.case_name: Nguyen v. Allied Pacific Construction Co.
citation.proposition: Supports the argument that compliance with safety requirements insulates a hirer from tort liability (cited in footnote 1).

citations.reasoning: Nguyen v. Allied Pacific Construction Co. (112 Cal.App.4th 845) involves the 'retained control' exception to the Privette doctrine, which actually makes it easier for a plaintiff to sue a hirer, rather than insulating them.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The case is well-known in California construction litigation for the 'Hooker/McKown' retained control analysis.

citation.text: Reeves v. Summit Engineering Group, 78 Cal.App.4th 531 (2000)
citation.proposition: Supports the argument that compliance with safety requirements insulates a hirer from tort liability (cited in footnote 1).

citations.reasoning: Reeves v. Summit Engineering Group (78 Cal.App.4th 531) is a case about professional negligence and the Privette doctrine, but it does not support the broad proposition that safety compliance provides absolute insulation.
citations.confidence_reasoning: The holding is more nuanced and focuses on the hirer's affirmative contribution to the injury.

citation.text: Rivera v. Cornerstone Grading & Paving, Inc., L.A. Superior Court Case No. BC-2019-33021
citation.case_name: Rivera v. Cornerstone Grading & Paving, Inc.
citation.citation: L.A. Superior Court Case No. BC-2019-33021
citation.proposition: Denying summary judgment where subcontractor retained control over safety equipment (cited in footnote 1).

citations.reasoning: Superior Court case numbers are not searchable in standard legal research databases (Westlaw/Lexis) in the same way as appellate opinions, and this specific case number format appears non-standard for L.A. Superior Court.
citations.confidence_reasoning: Trial court case numbers are internal and difficult to verify without specific court access.

facts.claim: On or about March 14, 2021, Rivera was working on a scaffolding assembly at the project site when a section of the scaffolding gave way.
facts.contradiction: The incident occurred on March 12, 2021, not March 14, 2021.
facts.confidence_reasoning: The date of the incident is an objective, verifiable fact stated in the official police report and confirmed by medical records.

facts.claim: Rivera was not wearing required personal protective equipment at the time of the incident, including fall-arrest equipment mandated by site safety protocols.
facts.contradiction: Witness testimony and the police report confirm Rivera was wearing a hard hat and safety harness, and the lanyard was attached but pulled free during the collapse.
facts.source_quote: Site supervisor Mark Ellison confirmed that Rivera was wearing a hard hat and harness consistent with site requirements at the time of the collapse. The harness lanyard was found still attached to Rivera but had pulled free from the scaffolding anchor point when the structure gave way.
facts.confidence_reasoning: The police report explicitly documents that the site supervisor confirmed the use of PPE, directly contradicting the MSJ's assertion.

facts.claim: The incident giving rise to this action occurred on March 14, 2021.
facts.confidence_reasoning: The date of the incident is an objective, verifiable fact stated in the official police report.
